title: Tapping for the Non-Linear Grief
subtitle: An EFT sequence for grief that returns when you thought it was over
excerpt: An EFT tapping sequence for the grief that doesn't follow a schedule: the wave that returns months or years later, the ordinary Tuesday that is suddenly unbearable. Taps through the exhaustion of non-linear grief and toward accepting that this is how grief actually works.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when grief has returned unexpectedly — particularly when you felt you had moved past it and finding yourself back in it is disorienting.

content.text: An EFT tapping sequence for the grief that comes back when you were sure it was done. The ambush grief, triggered by a song, an anniversary, a stranger's face. This sequence works on the exhaustion of grief that doesn't resolve and the shame of still being here in it.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though the grief came back when I thought I was past it, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I'm exhausted by grief that doesn't follow a timeline, I accept this is where I am right now.
Even though I thought I was done and I'm not done, I am willing to let this be here without it meaning I'm failing.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: I thought I was past this.
Side of Eye: The grief came back when I wasn't expecting it.
Under Eye: The day that felt fine and then suddenly wasn't.
Under Nose: The anger that grief doesn't run on a schedule.
Chin: The embarrassment that I'm still here in it.
Collarbone: The exhaustion of grief that comes in waves.
Under Arm: The feeling that this should be finished by now.
Top of Head: Being back in it when I thought I'd moved through it.
Reframe
Eyebrow: Grief doesn't move in one direction. Returning is part of the process.
Side of Eye: The wave came back because the love is still there. That makes sense.
Under Eye: Being here again is not failure. It is the non-linear nature of this.
Under Nose: There is no schedule that grief is behind on.
Chin: I don't have to be done with this before I'm allowed to be okay.
Collarbone: The waves get further apart. I am still in the process.
Under Arm: I am moving through this, even when it feels like I'm going backwards.
Top of Head: I can be here and still be okay. Both are true.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
